name: Parque Natural de las Marismas de Santoña, Victoria y Joyel – circular by the Muelle de los Frailes
name_original: Parque Natural de las Marismas de Santoña, Victoria y Joyel – circular por el Muelle de los Frailes
region_intro: Hiking around Escalante, Utah, offers diverse experiences within the Grand Staircase-Escalante National Monument, characterized by unique geological formations and extensive canyon systems. The region features numerous slot canyons, slickrock formations, and waterfalls, shaped by millions of years of erosion. This arid desert environment provides a range of trails from accessible strolls to challenging multi-day backpacking trips. Surreal rock formations such as hoodoos and natural arches define the landscape. Best hiking trails around Escalante The most popular hiking route is Monte Buciero: Lighthouses and Cliffs Trail, a 8.1 miles (13.0 km) difficult trail that takes 4 hours 35 minutes to complete. This route features significant elevation gain and coastal views. Another top favourite among local hikers is Faro del Caballo – circular by the Natural Park of the Marshes of Santoña, Victoria and Joyel, a difficult 6.0 miles (9.7 km) path. This trail offers varied terrain through a natural park. Local hikers also love the Parque Natural de las Marismas de Santoña, Victoria y Joyel – circular by the Muelle de los Frailes, a 4.7 miles (7.6 km) trail leading through a natural park, often completed in about 2 hours 13 minutes. Hiking around Escalante is defined by slot canyons, waterfalls, and unique rock formations. The network offers options for different ability levels, from family-friendly paths to challenging backcountry adventures. The routes in Escalante are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.36 stars from over 900 reviews. More than 6000 hikers have used komoot to explore Escalante's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many hiking trails are available in the Escalante area?
A: The Escalante region offers a vast network of trails, with over 260 hiking routes documented on komoot. These range from easy strolls to challenging multi-day adventures, catering to various skill levels.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect when hiking around Escalante?
A: Escalante's terrain is incredibly diverse, characterized by unique geological formations within the Grand Staircase-Escalante National Monument. You'll encounter extensive canyon systems, including famous slot canyons, surreal rock formations like hoodoos and natural arches, and even lush oases with waterfalls amidst the arid desert landscape.

Q: Are there family-friendly hiking options in Escalante?
A: Yes, Escalante has several excellent options for families. Trails like &lt;strong&gt;Willis Creek Slot Canyon&lt;/strong&gt; offer an easier and scenic introduction to slot canyon hiking. The popular &lt;strong&gt;Lower Calf Creek Falls&lt;/strong&gt; trail is also very accessible, leading to a magnificent waterfall and a lush oasis, making it a rewarding experience for all ages. The &lt;strong&gt;Escalante Petrified Forest State Park&lt;/strong&gt; has trails like the Petrified Forest Trail which are suitable for families.

Q: Can I bring my dog on the hiking trails in Escalante?
A: While many areas within the Grand Staircase-Escalante National Monument are dog-friendly, specific regulations can vary. It's always best to check the current rules for the particular trail or area you plan to visit. Generally, dogs should be kept on a leash to protect wildlife and ensure a pleasant experience for all hikers.

Q: Are there any hikes that feature waterfalls in Escalante?
A: Absolutely! Escalante is home to stunning waterfalls. The most famous is &lt;strong&gt;Lower Calf Creek Falls&lt;/strong&gt;, a popular and accessible hike leading to a beautiful 126-foot waterfall. You can also find &lt;strong&gt;Upper Calf Creek Falls&lt;/strong&gt; upstream, offering another picturesque cascade.

Q: What are the best slot canyons to explore in Escalante?
A: Escalante is renowned for its slot canyons. &lt;strong&gt;Peek-a-Boo and Spooky Gulch&lt;/strong&gt; are famous for their incredibly narrow passages and can be combined for an exciting loop. &lt;strong&gt;Zebra Slot Canyon&lt;/strong&gt; is known for its distinctive striped walls, while &lt;strong&gt;Willis Creek Slot Canyon&lt;/strong&gt; and &lt;strong&gt;Dry Fork Narrows&lt;/strong&gt; offer more beginner-friendly experiences. Be prepared for potential water pools in some slot canyons.

Q: What unique natural features can I explore besides canyons and waterfalls?
A: Beyond canyons and waterfalls, Escalante boasts surreal rock formations. &lt;strong&gt;Devil's Garden&lt;/strong&gt; features an array of hoodoos and natural arches like Metate Arch and Mano Arch. You can also visit the &lt;strong&gt;Escalante Petrified Forest State Park&lt;/strong&gt; to see vast collections of fossilized trees. For a truly unique sight, consider the challenging hike to the &lt;strong&gt;Cosmic Ashtray&lt;/strong&gt;, known for its distinctive rock pedestal. You can also explore the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4545693" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;La Ojerada Caves&lt;/a&gt; or enjoy views from &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5389027" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Marshes Viewpoint&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Do I need permits for hiking in Escalante?
A: For many day hikes in Escalante, permits are not required. However, for overnight backpacking trips, especially in popular wilderness areas or specific canyons like Coyote Gulch, permits are often necessary. It's crucial to check with the Bureau of Land Management (BLM) for the most current permit requirements before your trip. You can find more information on the official BLM website: &lt;a href="https://www.blm.gov/programs/national-conservation-lands/utah/grand-staircase-escalante-national-monument" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;blm.gov&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What is the best time of year to hike in Escalante?
A: The best times to hike in Escalante are typically spring (April to early June) and fall (September to October). During these seasons, temperatures are more moderate, making for comfortable hiking conditions. Summers can be extremely hot, and slot canyons may be prone to flash floods during monsoon season. Winters can bring cold temperatures and snow, especially at higher elevations.

Q: Are there any challenging or long-distance hikes in the Escalante area?
A: Yes, Escalante offers challenging options for experienced hikers. The region is known for multi-day backpacking trips into extensive canyon systems like &lt;strong&gt;Coyote Gulch&lt;/strong&gt;, which features waterfalls and natural arches. The &lt;strong&gt;Escalante River Trail&lt;/strong&gt; can also be undertaken as a long day trip or overnight adventure, often involving river crossings. These routes require careful planning and preparation.

Q: What do other hikers say about their experiences in Escalante?
A: The Escalante area is highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.37 stars from over 900 reviews. Hikers frequently praise the region's unique geological formations, the adventurous slot canyon experiences, and the stunning natural beauty of the waterfalls and rock formations. Many highlight the sense of remote wilderness and the diverse range of trails available.

Q: How accessible are the trailheads, and is parking available?
A: Many of Escalante's prime hiking destinations are accessed via &lt;strong&gt;Hole-in-the-Rock Road&lt;/strong&gt;, a well-known dirt road that branches off Scenic Byway 12. While Scenic Byway 12 is paved, Hole-in-the-Rock Road can be rough and may require a high-clearance vehicle, especially after rain. Most popular trailheads have designated parking areas, but these can fill up quickly during peak seasons, so arriving early is recommended.

Q: Are there any circular hiking routes in Escalante?
A: While many trails in Escalante are out-and-back, some offer loop possibilities or can be combined for circular experiences. For instance, &lt;strong&gt;Peek-a-Boo and Spooky Gulch&lt;/strong&gt; are often hiked as a loop. Exploring areas like &lt;strong&gt;Devil's Garden&lt;/strong&gt; also allows for free exploration in a circular fashion around its unique rock formations.
tips: Located behind the Montehano Convent and on the Escalante estuary, this small pier is in a privileged place for its tranquility and views of the entire Santoña marsh.

Belonging to the Order of the Capuchin Fathers, this beautiful monastery was founded by the Franciscans at the beginning of the 14th century. Later it was enlarged until it was sold after the French invasions of the 19th century and finally recovered later by the Order.
A pity that only the ruins of the castle remain, but what remains intact are the beautiful views from the top of Montehano hill where the ruins are located.

waypoints.name: Monastery of San Sebastián de Montehano
